features.name: Global HTTP(S) Load Balancing
features.description: Acts as a single global IP address entry point, routing requests to the nearest healthy backend region.

features.name: Cloud CDN Edge Caching
features.description: Caches static web assets at Google's global Edge Points of Presence (PoPs) to minimize latency.

features.name: Globally Redundant DNS
features.description: Translates domain names to IP addresses globally using Google's redundant anycast nameserver network.

iamRoles.description: Full access to configure load balancers, SSL certificates, and URL maps.
Permissions to create, modify, and delete DNS zones, records, and policies.

cliExamples.description: Create a global static external IP address for a load balancer
Create a DNS managed zone for domain routing
cliExamples.command:
gcloud dns managed-zones create cloud-dns-zone \
  --dns-name="example.com." \
  --description="My domain zone"

design: Cloud Load Balancing distributes traffic across multiple virtual machines, containers, or serverless backends.
Global HTTP(S) Load Balancer is a Layer 7 proxy using a single Anycast IP, supporting URL path routing.
SSL Proxy and TCP Proxy are global Layer 4 load balancers for non-HTTP traffic, terminating SSL at edge.
Network Load Balancer (NLB) is a regional, non-proxy Layer 4 load balancer that preserves client IP addresses.
Cloud CDN caches static content close to users at Google edge points, integrating with HTTP(S) load balancers.
Cloud DNS provides highly scalable, low-latency domain name resolution using anycast routing.
Configure DNS routing policies (geo-routing, failover) to manage global traffic destination paths.
Use URL maps to define routing rules, sending traffic to specific backend buckets or VM groups.
security: Integrate Cloud Armor with HTTP(S) Load Balancers to defend against Layer 7 OWASP Top 10 and DDoS attacks.
Terminate SSL/TLS connections at the load balancer using Google-managed SSL certificates with automatic renewal.
Access control to backend resources can be restricted by enabling Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP) on the load balancer.
Enable DNSSEC (Domain Name System Security Extensions) in Cloud DNS to prevent cache poisoning and spoofing.
Configure SSL policies on the load balancer to restrict weak ciphers and enforce TLS version limits.
Protect backend servers by placing them in private subnets, allowing traffic only from the load balancer proxy IPs.
Use backend service signing keys to secure CDN cache configurations (signed URLs/cookies).
Enable HTTPS-only redirection at the load balancer level to enforce transport encryption.
reliability: Global load balancers automatically route traffic around regional outages to healthy backend regions.
Configure multi-region backend services with capacity limits, letting the load balancer spill over traffic automatically.
Managed health checks verify VM/container readiness, immediately draining connections from failed nodes.
Cloud DNS has an availability SLA of 100% supported by Google's global nameserver network.
Configure DNS failover policies to route users to backup cloud resources during primary application failures.
Cloud CDN uses automatic cache bypass during backend failures to ensure dynamic content delivery.
Load balancers handle sudden traffic spikes gracefully without requiring warming.
Use session affinity settings to bind client requests to the same backend node when state is required.
cost: Load balancing charges are based on forwarding rules, ingress data processed (GB), and backend connections.
Cloud CDN bills for cache egress network fees, cache fill operations, and cache lookup requests.
Cloud DNS charges per managed zone and for millions of DNS queries processed per month.
Cloud CDN significantly reduces network egress billing by serving cached files from edge points instead of backend VMs.
Use Google-managed SSL certificates to eliminate the purchase and manual renewal cost of certificates.
Consolidate multiple domains under a single global load balancer using URL host rules to minimize rule charges.
Configure CDN cache TTLs (Time to Live) to maximize cache hit ratios, reducing backend compute costs.
Delete unused forwarding rules and static IP reservations to avoid idle network fees.
